title-group.article-title: Physical, cognitive, and mental health impacts of Omicron reinfection in patients with original SARS-CoV-2 infection: a community-based observational study

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Epidemiological and clinical evidence suggests that severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) reinfection is a complication in a proportion of patients reporting ongoing health issues. However, most studies in the field of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection have focused only on self-reported symptoms and lacked long-term objective measurements. This study aimed to estimate the pattern of chronic symptoms of Omicron reinfection in patients with original SARS-CoV-2 infection by comprehensively assessments 3 years after recovery.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This community-based observational study was conducted in Wuhan, China, between January and April in 2023. All participants were recruited from community and invited to participate the interview and examination in a hospital. The subjective multi-system symptoms were self-reported. The objective radiological features and laboratory data were assessed by measuring blood inflammation and performing chest computed tomography (CT) and pulse oxygen saturation.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Among 1438 individuals who participated in the study, 144 were infected with the original variant only in 2020, 980 were Omicron-infected in 2023, 215 were reinfected both in 2020 and 2023, and 99 were never infected. Compared with the non-infection group, the reinfection (odds ratio (OR), 5.15 [95% confidence intervals (CIs), 2.96–8.96]) group was associated with the highest risk of any of chronic physical symptoms, followed by the Omicron infection (3.45 [2.19–5.44]) and original variant infection (2.90 [1.63–5.15]) groups. Compared with the non-infection group, the reinfection (4.05 [2.30–7.14]), Omicron infection (3.72 [2.26–6.11]), and original variant infection (2.71 [1.48–4.95]) groups were associated with an increased risk of any of chronic mental symptoms. Moreover, of all groups, the reinfection group reported the highest seropositivity rate for C-reactive protein, and the highest prevalence rates of ground glass opacities on chest CT and hypoxaemia.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Our results suggest that reinfection may be a risk factor for long COVID conditions. These findings provide information for the clinical management and healthcare services of long COVID and SARS-CoV-2 reinfection and highlight the importance taking necessary action to prepare for a future pandemic. The long-term follow-up will be needed to verify the impact of different SARS-CoV-2 infection status on long COVID in the future.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12916-025-04322-5.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Background
sec.p: With the changes in policy and wide circulation of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2), the majority of global population has been infected at least once [1]. Global all-cause mortality rates have markedly increased and Life expectancy has decreased during the Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic in 2020 and 2021 [2]. Long COVID, which is referred to as long-haul COVID-19 or post-acute COVID-19 syndrome, appears to be a persistent symptom in multiple organ systems for at least 2 months after 3 months from the onset of COVID-19, and has been associated with a substantial burden on public health care systems [3]. The original variant is more likely to precede long COVID than the other variants [4, 5]. Previous studies have also found a high risk of moderate or severe sequelae after 20-month follow-up in patients infected with the original SARS-CoV-2 variant [6, 7]. However, few studies have comprehensively assessed the long-term multisystem outcomes of reinfection in patients with the original SARS-CoV-2 infection 3 years after recovery. Most previous studies were Limited to short follow-up durations and did not include control groups. Therefore, detailed information about long COVID over the 3 years after recovery from the original variant is required to provide insights into the comprehensive management and treatment of COVID-19.

SARS-CoV-2 has been mutating rapidly, and new variants replace older patients every few months. Therefore, the risk of reinfection is particularly high with the Omicron variant, which has a marked ability to evade immunity against previous infection [8, 9]. However, there is no consensus on the patterns of acute and chronic symptoms caused by reinfection. Some studies have indicated that previous infections are effective in decreasing the severity of reinfection [10, 11]. A meta-analysis found that protection from past SARS-CoV-2 infections against subsequent severe symptoms was universally high for the ancestral, Alpha, Beta, Delta, and Omicron variants during the acute phase [10]. In contrast, some studies have raised concerns about the high risk of sequelae and development of respiratory diseases among participants with SARS-CoV-2 reinfection [12, 13]. A large cohort study also showed that, compared with primary infection, reinfection was associated with an increased risk of asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, interstitial lung disease, and lung cancer [12]. Moreover, the cumulative risks and burdens of sequelae in patients with reinfection increased according to the number of infections and the risks of sequelae that persisted in the post-acute phase after 6 months [13]. Overall, these inconsistent findings prompt further research to understand how outcomes potentially differ between different variants and reinfection combinations of SARS-CoV-2, particularly over a long period.

Despite the availability of clinical evidence regarding symptoms or specific organ functions, few studies have focused on the radiological and laboratory characteristics of patients reinfected with different SARS-CoV-2 variants. SARS-CoV-2 persists in various body tissues and organs via circulating autoantibodies months after infection [14, 15]. Many inflammation biomarkers, including C-reactive protein (CRP) [16], β2-microglobulin [17], and interleukin-6 (IL-6) [18], were associated with long COVID. Analysis of computed tomography (CT) data following up to at least 6 months after infection showed that 34% and 32% of survivors had persistent ground glass opacities and pulmonary fibrosis, respectively [19]. Impaired health as a result of the first infection is associated with an increased risk of adverse health consequence of reinfection [20]. Although the last immunity-conferring event in people who have previously been infected with SARS-CoV-2 may help explain the different physical and neuropsychological symptoms during the acute and chronic phase [13, 21], the mechanisms underlying the pathogenesis of reinfection and associated long COVID remain unclear. Evidence based on a comprehensive assessment of objective radiological features and laboratory data is needed to further our understanding of the long-term impact of reinfection in patients with the original SARS-CoV-2 infection. Therefore, this study aimed to estimate the pattern of acute and long-term chronic symptoms of Omicron reinfection in patients with the original SARS-CoV-2 infection by comprehensively assessing with subjective symptom questionnaires and objective radiological and laboratory data, including blood inflammation (CRP, IL-6, procalcitonin (PCT), IgG, IgM), chest CT, electrocardiogram (ECG), blood pressure, heart rate, and pulse oxygen saturation (SpO2).

sec.title: Study design and participants
sec.p: This community-based observational study was conducted at Wuchang Hospital and followed the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) reporting guidelines and statements (Additional file 1: Table S1). The Ethics Committee of Peking University Sixth Hospital (Institute of Mental Health) approved this study. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to the study procedures.

We recruited community-dwelling residents aged 15 years or older from a district in Wuhan, China, between 10 January and 28 April 2023. Eligible participants were invited by the staff from a Center for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) based on their history of COVID-19 exposure. Participants were considered eligible if they met any of the following criteria: (1) infected with SARS-CoV-2 during the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic (December 2019 to May 2020); (2) infected during the Omicron wave (from June 2022 onwards); (3) infected during both the first wave and the Omicron wave. Participants never infected were invited to take part in this investigation as control group. All participants were investigated by questionnaires self-reported and physical examination, including demographic characteristics, clinical information, questionnaires and blood samples, at Wuchang Hospital. SARS-CoV-2 infection was confirmed by antigen test, nucleic acid amplification test, or clinician diagnosis. We categorised the participants into four groups: those who had original SARS-CoV-2 infection, those who had Omicron infection, those who had reinfection, and those with non-infection as the control group. Participants who were infected with the original variant of SARS-CoV-2 during the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic from December 2019 to May 2020 but did not report SARS-CoV-2 infection during the Omicron wave from June 2022 onwards were defined as the original variant infection group. Participants who were only infected with SARS-CoV-2 during the Omicron wave from June 2022 onwards but did not report the original variant infection were defined as the Omicron infection group. The participants who reinfected with SARS-CoV-2 during both the first and Omicron waves were defined as the reinfection group. As of the data collection phase, participants with non-infection were those had never been diagnosed with COVID-19 by a positive SARS-CoV-2 test (antigen test or nucleic acid amplification test). Furthermore, in this study, plasma samples of all patients were collected. Anti-nucleocapsid immunoglobulin (Ig)G of SARS-CoV-2 indicates not only previous infection with the virus, but also immune responses induced by inactivated COVID-19 vaccines, which are the most widely used vaccine type in China. COVID-19 vaccination has been widely administered among the Chinese population. Therefore, individuals were not excluded from the never infection group based on IgG antibody test results. In contrast, because anti-nucleocapsid IgM positivity typically reflects recent SARS-CoV-2 infection, participants with positive anti-nucleocapsid IgM were excluded from the never infection group. Individuals with SARS-CoV-2 infection between June 2020 and May 2022 and those with invalid questionnaires were excluded.

sec.title: Measurements
sec.p: Demographic characteristics included age, sex, education, marital status, occupation, monthly family income, living area, and co-residential partners. Data on drinking, smoking, physical activity intensity, SARS-CoV-2 vaccination doses, the time from the last COVID-19 vaccination to investigation, history of physical diseases, history of mental disorders, and family history of mental disorders were also collected.

The clinical information on acute symptoms and chronic physical sequelae after SARS-CoV-2 infection was collected by trained interviewers with symptom questionnaires (Additional file 1: Table S2). Symptoms within 2 months of infection were defined as acute symptoms, whereas chronic symptoms lasted for at least 2 months and more after infection. Fifty acute symptoms and their severity in eight organ systems (general, respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, ophthalmic, neurological, psychological, and dermatological systems) after infection with SARS-CoV-2 during the Omicron infection wave were collected from participants with Omicron infection and reinfection.

Chronic mental health included depression symptoms measured using the Patient Health Questionaire-9 (PHQ-9) [22], anxiety symptoms measured using the Generalised Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7) [23], insomnia symptoms measured using the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) [24], acute stress symptoms measured using the Acute Stress Disorder Scale (ASDS) [25], and PTSD symptoms measured using the PTSD checklist for the DSM-5 (PCL-5) [26]. The total scores of these scales were interpreted as follows: PHQ-9, normal (0–4), mild (5–9), moderate (10–14), and severe (≥ 15) depression symptoms; GAD-7, normal (0–4), mild (5–9), moderate (10–14), and severe (≥ 15) anxiety symptoms; ISI, normal (0–7), subthreshold (8–14), moderate (15–21), and severe (≥ 22) insomnia symptoms; ASDS, a dissociative cluster score ≥ 9 and cumulative re-experiencing, avoidance, and arousal cluster scores ≥ 28. We used the following cut-off scores as having depression (PHQ-9 ≥ 5), anxiety (GAD-7 ≥ 5), insomnia (ISI ≥ 8), or PTSD symptoms (PCL-5 ≥ 33). Chronic cognitive function was assessed using the Abbreviated Cognitive Failure Questionnaire-14 (CFQ-14) [27]. The CFQ-14 measures daily Life cognitive failures, which has 14 questions with a 5-point Likert scale resulting in a factored score ranging from 0 to 100, with a score of ≥ 43 indicating cognitive failures [28]. Chronic fatigue was also assessed using the 14-item Fatigue Scale, in which each item was scored as 0 (no fatigue-related problem) or 1 (has fatigue-related problem), and the total score was calculated to range from 0 to 14 [29]. Quality of life was assessed using the EuroQol five-dimensional three-level (EQ-5D-3L) questionnaire and EuroQol Visual Analogue Scale (EQ-VAS) [30]. Blood samples were collected to test for inflammation (the IgM and IgG antibodies against the nucleoprotein of SARS-CoV-2, CRP, IL-6, and PCT). In addition, CT, ECG, blood pressure, heart rate, and SpO2 characteristics were investigated.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: In this study, continuous variables were presented as mean (standard deviation [SD]) or median (interquartile range [IQR]), along with counts and percentages for binary and categorical variables. We compared the difference in acute symptoms and their severity between the two groups (Omicron and reinfection groups) and compared the chronic symptoms, blood inflammation, CT, and ECG characteristics in the four groups by COVID-19 infection status (never infected, original SARS-CoV-2 infection, Omicron infection, and reinfection). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and t-test were used for normally distributed continuous data, and Kruskal–Wallis tests were used for non-normally distributed data to test the differences in chronic symptoms. The χ2 test was used for the comparison of proportions in acute symptoms and chronic symptoms. Unordered polytomous logistic regression models were used to estimate the odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the association between SARS-CoV-2 infection status and chronic symptoms, cognitive function, and mental health outcomes after adjusting for Confounder-1, which included age, sex, history of physical diseases, history of mental disorders, family history of mental disorders, education, marital status, occupation, monthly family income, living area, co-residential partners, drinking, smoking, intensity of physical activity, COVID-19 vaccination doses, and the time from the last COVID-19 vaccination to investigation. We also used binary multiple logistic regression models to investigate the association between SARS-CoV-2 infection status and acute symptom outcomes after adjusting for Confounder-2 (Confounder-1 plus the time from the occurrence of Omicron infection to investigation). Statistically significant differences were those with P values of less than 0.05 for two-tailed tests. Several sensitivity analyses on targeting lab-confirmed COVID-19 patients were performed to check the robustness of the primary results. All analyses were performed using Stata MP version 17 and R version 4.3.1.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p:
A total of 1526 participants were invited to participate in our Survey, with 1489 participants willing to participate and 37 not completing the questionnaires. Finally, 1438 participants were eligible for the Survey, and 51 were excluded because of invalid questionnaires. The response rate was 97.6% (1489/1526) and the effective rate was 96.6% (1,438/1,489) (Fig. 1). The demographic and vaccination characteristics of the participants are shown in Table 1. The mean age of the 1438 participants was 56.0 years (SD, 14.7 years), and 862 (59.9%) were female. Among the 1438 eligible participants, 144 (10.0%) participants had only original variant infection, 980 (68.1%) participants had only Omicron infection, 215 (15.0%) participants were reinfected with both original and Omicron variants, and 99 (6.9%) participants were never infected as a control group. No difference was found in the rates of hospitalisation after original variant infection between the original variant infection (82.6%) and reinfection groups (80.5%, P = 0.604). Similarly, we found no difference in the rates of hospitalisation after Omicron infection between the Omicron infection (2.9%) and reinfection groups (2.3%, P = 0.667). In addition, the results showed that no difference in the severity of acute symptoms between the original variant infection (asymptomatic 9.0%, mild 29.2%, moderate 37.5%, severe 18.8%, and unknown 5.5%) and reinfection groups (asymptomatic 6.1%, mild 20.0%, moderate 47.9%, severe 20.9%, and unknown 5.1%; P = 0.165). Furthermore, the time from the occurrence of original variant infection to investigation was longer in the original variant infection group (mean ± SD: 37.5 ± 2.9 months) than in the reinfection group (36.3 ± 4.6 months; P = 0.006). The time from the occurrence of Omicron infection to investigation was longer in the reinfection group (74.0 ± 29.8 days) than in the Omicron infection group (52.0 ± 16.8 days; P < 0.001). The positive rates of antigen or nucleic acid amplification tests in the reinfection, Omicron infection, original variant infection and control groups were 100.0%, 100.0%, 100.0%, and 0.0%, respectively (P < 0.001).Fig. 1
Flow chart of the study. A total of 1,526 participants were invited in our Survey, with 1,489 participants willing to participate in the Survey and 37 not participating in the Survey. A final count of 1,489 participants eligible for Survey with the exclusion of 51 individuals were excluded because of invalid questionnaire. The response rate: 1489 eligible survivors included in analysis/1526 eligible survivors invited = 97.6%. The effective rate: 1438 eligible questionnaires/1489 completed questionnaires = 96.6%Table 1

sec.title: The impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection on acute symptoms
sec.p:
The acute symptom characteristics of participants with Omicron infection and reinfection after the emergence of the Omicron wave are shown in Fig. 2 and Additional file 1: Table S3. Compared with participants with Omicron infection, the reinfection group reported lower prevalence rates of any of acute symptoms (87.0% vs 94.1%), general symptoms (80.5% vs 88.5%), respiratory symptoms (79.5% vs 89.7%), cardiovascular symptoms (14.9% vs 21.9%), gastrointestinal symptoms (47.4% vs 60.3%), ophthalmic symptoms (25.1% vs 33.6%), neurological symptoms (59.5% vs 76.1%), and psychological symptoms (47.0% vs 56.0%; all P < 0.05). Compared to reinfection group, Omicron infection group reported higher rates of severe general, respiratory, and neurological symptoms that associated with Omicron infection (all P < 0.05). No difference was found in the rates of cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, ophthalmic, psychological dermatological symptoms between Omicron infection and reinfection groups (all P > 0.05). The reinfection group also reported lower rates in medical services (35.8% vs. 77.0%; P < 0.001) than did the Omicron infection group. Sensitivity analyses on targeting lab-confirmed COVID-19 patients showed that compared with participants with Omicron infection, the reinfection group reported lower rates of acute neurological symptoms, and medical services (all P < 0.05; Additional file 1: Table S4). However, binary multiple logistic regression analysis showed that compared with those with Omicron infection, the reinfection group was not associated with a protective effect against any of acute symptoms after adjusting for potential confounders (Additional file 1: Table S5). Sensitivity analyses on targeting lab-confirmed COVID-19 patients revealed similar results (Additional file 1: Table S6). Furthermore, unordered polytomous logistic regression analysis showed no significant association between the number of COVID-19 vaccination doses and risk of development of any of acute symptoms after adjusting for potential confounders. However, compared to no vaccination, a time interval of less than 6 months from the last COVID-19 vaccination to investigation was associated with a significantly lower risk of developing any acute symptoms after adjusting for potential confounders. This association was not observed when the time interval exceeded 6 months (Additional file 1: Table S7).Fig. 2
Prevalence of acute symptoms of participants with Omicron infection and reinfection after the emergence of omicron wave

sec.title: The impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection on chronic physical symptoms
sec.p:
The comparisons of chronic symptoms among the four groups are shown in Table 2. Compared with the control group, reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups reported higher rates of any of chronic (78.1%, 69.2% and 68.1% vs 39.4%; P < 0.001), general (54.9%, 42.2% and 38.2% vs 13.1%; P < 0.001), respiratory (36.3%, 40.3% and 33.3% vs 16.2%; P < 0.001), cardiovascular (27.4%, 13.7% and 19.4% vs 4.0%; P < 0.001), ophthalmic (15.3%, 8.1% and 11.8% vs 6.1%; P = 0.004), neurological (50.2%, 32.3% and 48.6% vs 20.2%; P < 0.001, and dermatological (20.5%, 11.5% and 13.2% vs 11.1%; P = 0.005) symptoms. Chronic fatigue/tiredness is the most common symptom. The score of fatigue (7.1, 6.4 and 6.3 vs 3.7; P < 0.001; Table 2) and the proportion of fatigue were higher in the reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups than in the control group (49.3%, 36.9%, and 31.9% vs. 11.1%; P < 0.001; Additional file 1: Table S8). In the sensitivity analyses on targeting lab-confirmed COVID-19 patients, we found that compared with the control group, the reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups still reported higher rates of any of chronic physical symptoms (all P < 0.05; Additional file 1: Table S9). Unordered polytomous logistic regression analysis showed that compared with the control group and adjusted for potential confounders, reinfection (OR, 5.15 [95% CI, 2.96–8.96]; P < 0.001), Omicron infection (3.45 [2.19–5.44]; P < 0.001), and original variant infection (2.90 [1.63–5.15]; P < 0.001) groups were associated with increased risk of any of chronic physical symptoms. In addition, compared to the control group, the reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups were also associated with increased risks of chronic general, respiratory, cardiovascular, and neurological symptoms after adjusting for potential confounders (Fig. 3A). The sensitivity analyses on targeting lab-confirmed COVID-19 patients illustrated similar results (Additional file 1: Table S10). Moreover, unordered polytomous multiple logistic regression analysis showed that the risk of any of chronic physical symptoms was not significantly associated with the number of COVID-19 vaccination doses or the time from the last COVID-19 vaccination to investigation, after adjusting for potential confounders (Additional file 1: Table S11). sec.title: The impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection on chronic mental symptoms and cognitive impairment
sec.p: Table 2 shows the chronic mental, cognitive, and fatigue symptoms and health-related quality of life of the four groups. Compared with the controls, the reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups had higher rates of any of chronic mental symptoms (61.4%, 56.1% and 54.9% vs 30.3%; P < 0.001), mild-to-severe anxiety (35.3%, 31.7% and 27.8% vs 13.1%; P = 0.015), mild-to-severe depression (41.9%, 40.8% and 38.9% vs 17.2%; P < 0.001), mild-to-severe insomnia (47.9%, 40.2% and 42.4% vs 20.2%; P < 0.001), acute stress/PTSD symptoms (22.3%, 8.1% and 18.8% vs 6.1%; P < 0.001), and cognitive failure (20.5%, 15.2% and 19.4% vs 8.1%; P = 0.023). The quality of life score (68.1, 70.1 and 67.5 vs 77.1; P = 0.003) was significantly lower in the reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups than in the control group (Table 2). The sensitivity analyses on targeting lab-confirmed COVID-19 patients found that compared with the control group, the reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups still reported higher rates of any of chronic mental symptoms (all P < 0.05; Additional file 1: Table S9). Furthermore, unordered polytomous logistic regression analysis showed that compared with the control group and adjusted for potential confounders, the original variant infection group (2.71 [1.48–4.95]; P = 0.001), Omicron infection (3.72 [2.26–6.11]; P < 0.001), and reinfection (4.05 [2.30–7.14]; P < 0.001) groups were associated with increased risk of any of chronic mental symptoms, and specific chronic neuropsychiatric symptoms, including anxiety, depression, insomnia, and cognitive failure. Moreover, compared with the control group, the reinfection group was associated with an increased risk of acute stress/PTSD symptoms (Fig. 3B). In the sensitivity analyses on targeting lab-confirmed COVID-19 patients, we found that compared with the control group, the reinfection, Omicron infection, and original variant infection groups were still associated with increased risk of any of chronic mental symptoms (all P < 0.05; Additional file 1: Table S10). Moreover, unordered polytomous logistic regression analysis showed that the risks of any of chronic mental symptoms and cognitive impairment were not significantly associated with the number of COVID-19 vaccination doses or the time from the last COVID-19 vaccination to investigation, after adjusting for potential confounders (Additional file 1: Table S11).

sec.title: The impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection on laboratory characteristics
sec.p:
The laboratory characteristics of the four groups are presented in Table 3. The Omicron infection group had higher seropositivity rates for IgG (99.3%) and IgM (22.8%) against the nucleoprotein of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein than the other groups (all P < 0.001). The seropositivity rate for CRP was significantly highest in the reinfection group than in the other groups (2.4%; P = 0.014). This study showed no difference in the seropositivity rates for IL-6 and PCT among the four groups. Additionally, chest CT showed a higher rate of ground-glass opacities in participants with reinfection (9.5%) and original variant infection (9.2%) than in the Omicron infection (5.9%) and control groups (2.0%; P = 0.036). As for the measurement of cardiovascular function, the proportion of hypoxaemia was significantly higher in the reinfection (3.8%) and original variant infection (4.4%) groups than in the Omicron infection (0.6%) and control groups (2.0%; P < 0.001), but no difference in abnormal ECGs, hypertension, bradycardia, and tachycardia was observed in all groups. sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to investigate the pattern of acute and long-term health outcomes of Omicron reinfection in patients infected with the original SARS-CoV-2 infection by a comprehensive assessment of subjective multi-system symptom questionnaires and objective laboratory characteristics, including blood inflammation, CT, ECG, blood pressure, heart rate, and SpO2. There was no difference in the rate or severity of acute symptoms between the Omicron infection and reinfection groups. However, this community-based observational study showed that reinfection may be a risk factor for long COVID conditions. Moreover, of all groups, the reinfection and original variant infection groups reported higher rates of ground glass opacity on chest CT and hypoxaemia; the reinfection group reported the highest rates of seropositivity for CRP; the Omicron infection group reported the highest rates of seropositivity for IgG and IgM. There were no differences in other laboratory characteristics among the four groups, including seropositivity for IL-6 and PCT, ECG, blood pressure, and heart rate. These findings provide information for the prevention and management of long COVID during the post-pandemic era.

It is estimated that more than half a billion people worldwide are infected with SARS-CoV-2 at least once [13], and 65% of the population has experienced multiple infections [31]. For the large and growing number of people who encountered their first infection, the question of whether reinfection carries additional health risks remains a priority. In this study, we found no difference in acute symptoms and severity between the reinfection and Omicron infection groups. This finding is partially similar to a previous finding that indicated no significant difference in the severity of the acute symptoms of new-onset SARS-CoV-2 infection between primary infection and reinfection [32]. Additionally, some studies have found effective protection of a previous infection against reinfection with Alpha, Beta, Delta, and Omicron variants of SARS-CoV-2 [8, 33, 34]. However, another cohort study showed that compared to the no reinfection group, the reinfection group was associated with a 2.17-fold higher risk of death, a 3.32-fold higher risk of hospitalisation, and increased risks of sequelae in multiple organ systems in both the acute and lone-term phase [13]. These inconsistent findings demonstrate the necessity for large cohort studies to provide more definitive evidence on the acute impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection.

The high transmissibility of the Omicron variant highlights the importance of investigating the clinical features of reinfection with the original and Omicron variants. Our study showed that, compared with participants without infection, patients reinfected with the original and Omicron variants (74 days after reinfection) had the highest risk of any of chronic physical and neuropsychiatric symptoms, followed by patients with Omicron infection (52 days after infection) and patients with the original variant infection (37.5 months after infection). A previous cohort study of COVID-19 survivors also found that, compared with the omicron infection, the identified new-onset neurological symptoms were associated with the original variant infection [35]. Patients with the original variant infection have a reduced symptom burden, but those with severe symptoms requiring intensive care unit admission still have a high risk of persistent symptoms 2 years after hospital discharge [36, 37]. Among COVID-19 Survivors 3 years after the original variant infection, participants with long COVID were associated with re-infection during the Omicron wave compared with participants without long COVID [7]. Our findings provide valuable information for understanding the long-term health outcomes of reinfection with the original and Omicron variants.

Anti-inflammatory activity and energy metabolism in response to Omicron infection were similar to those in the original variant infection. However, compared with the original variant, Omicron infection has an increased antioxidant capacity and liver detoxification and causes weaker inflammatory alterations and immune response [38]. Our study showed that the rate of seropositivity of IgG was significantly higher in the Omicron infection, reinfection, and original variant infection groups than that in the control group. Compared to vaccination-matched controls, CD4 + and CD8 + T cells from individuals with long COVID produce significantly more IgG against the SARS-CoV-2 spike and S1, indicating that long COVID may result from chronic inflammation [39, 40]. The observation of an elevated rate of seropositivity of CRP in reinfection than in Omicron infection also suggests that persistent CRP may be a feature of long COVID [16]. Consistent with our results, no significant differences in inflammatory markers (such as leukocytes, neutrophils, lymphocytes, and IL-6) were found between the survivors of SARS-CoV-2 and healthy controls [41]. Moreover, the prevalences rates of CT abnormalities and lowered SpO2 were higher in the reinfection and original variant infection than in the Omicron infection. These differences may be because the original variant damages the epithelial compartment in the lower airways, when Omicron contributes to upper airway infection [38, 42–44]. With the variation in SARS-CoV-2 variants, the immunity mechanism of long COVID has become increasingly intricate, and further studies will be needed to explore the immunological underpinnings of reinfection and long COVID.

Owing to these limitations, these findings should be interpreted with caution. First, the causal relationships should be interpreted with caution, and their generalisability remains to be investigated because this was a single-centre, community-based observational study. Second, there is the possibility of measurement error because the estimated symptoms were assessed by trained interviewers with symptom questionnaires, even though all symptoms were subjected to quality assurance measures. Third, the difference in time from the occurrence of the first symptom to the investigation between Omicron infection and reinfection may contribute to recall bias which results in increased recorded prevalence of acute symptoms, and their severity is higher in Omicron infection than in reinfection. We performed a multiple logistic regression analysis and found that Omicron infection was not associated with an increased risk of any of acute symptoms or severity of acute symptoms after adjusting for potential confounders. Forth, COVID-19 acute-phase symptoms were defined as symptoms that appear up to 4 weeks after the onset of illness. some previous studies defined long-term complications of SARS-CoV-2 infection beyond 4 weeks after initial infection [45, 46]. However, according to the 2024 National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM) definition and the study published by the World Health Organization (WHO), long COVID was defined as the symptoms that usually occur after 3 months from the onset of the probable or confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection [47–49]. In this study, acute symptoms were defined as symptoms within 2 months of infection, whereas chronic symptoms lasted for at least 2 months and more after infection. The follow-up periods were relatively short in the Omicron infection group and the reinfection group (median, 52 days and 74 days, respectively), which might have resulted in the limitations of representative and interpretation. This study also lacks detailed information on the duration and persistence of individual symptoms, future longitudinal studies should incorporate time-resolved symptom tracking to better capture the trajectory of long COVID conditions. Fifth, despite the use of antigen test, nucleic acid amplification test and anti-nucleocapsid IgM testing to identify never-infected individuals, the widespread administration of vaccines among participants limited the ability to use anti-nucleocapsid IgG serology for exclusion. As a result, the never infection group may still include individuals with previous asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infections that went undetected, highlighting the need for more rigorous classification criteria in future studies. Sixth, the small sample size of participants could have affected the reliability of the association between SARS-CoV-2 reinfection and the risk of developing chronic symptoms. Due to the nature of our recruitment strategy, individuals with severe or fatal outcomes were less likely or unable to participate. Although this subgroup may represent a relatively small fraction of the total infected population, their exclusion may lead to an underestimation of the burden and persistence of severe symptoms. Individuals with relevant acute symptoms or symptoms of long COVID or post COVID syndrome were more likely to participate in this study. The properties of the population, such as the mean age or the proportion of retirees, are not representative of the whole population of Wuhan. While we acknowledge that the representativeness of the sample is limited and that selection bias may affect the assessment of associations between SARS-CoV-2 reinfection and clinical outcomes, it is noteworthy that participants with different infection statuses were recruited from the same community by CDC. The shared lifestyle, cultural background, and social environment across participants offer a relatively homogeneous context, which may mitigate some sources of bias and support internal comparisons. The large cohort studies of patients with SARS-CoV-2 reinfection will be needed to verify the impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection on long COVID in the future.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Our observations suggest that reinfection may be a risk factor for long COVID conditions. This work provides evidence for a comprehensive assessment of subjective multi-system symptom questionnaires and objective radiological and laboratory tests to better understand the long-term impact of reinfection in patients with the original SARS-CoV-2 infection. The chest CT features and pulse oxygen saturation abnormalities suggested that the long-term lung function impairment three-year after the original SARS-CoV-2 infection. These findings are crucial for informing policy decisions on clinical management and healthcare services of long COVID and SARS-CoV-2 reinfection and highlight the importance of taking necessary action to prevention the long term impact in the future. Further research is required to develop interventions that mitigate the physical, cognitive, and mental sequelae associated with SARS-CoV-2 reinfection and to promote well-being.

SS, ZJ, LS, XL1, ZZ, HM, NG, YZ1, ML, SW, YZ2, NZ, SN, WY, KY, and YS designed the questionnaire. SS, ZJ, XL1, ZZ HM, NG, ML, SW, XZ, AZ, CT, YZ2, NZ, SN, YH, and YL contributed to recruiting participants and the data collection. SS, ZJ, LS, NZ and YZ1 analysed the data and wrote the initial draft of the manuscript. SS, XL1, JS, XL2, YB and LL commented on and revised the manuscript. XL2, YB and LL finalised the manuscript, with contributions from all authors. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
